---
title: "Towards translational and mechanistic whole-brain simulations with The Virtual Brain"
subtitle: "Track 4 Workshop"
author:
  - "Leon Stefanovski^1^"
  - "Marius Pille^1,2^"
  - "Leon Martin^1,2^"
affiliations:
  - id: charite
    name: "Charité University Medicine Berlin"
  - id: bih
    name: "Berlin Institute of Health at Charité University Medicine"
date: 2026-05-12
bibliography: ../bibliography.bib
format:
  pdf:
    toc: true
    number-sections: false
  docx:
    toc: true
    number-sections: false
---

**Affiliations:** ^1^ Charité University Medicine Berlin; ^2^ Berlin Institute of Health at Charité University Medicine

**Workshop Dates:** 12-13 May 2026

## About

In this workshop, participants will learn core concepts and methods for personalized whole-brain network modeling using The Virtual Brain (TVB). The workflow combines individualized model definition, simulation, and analysis with ontology-driven model specification and optimization tooling [@Martin2025; @Pille2025].

The Virtual Brain Ontology (TVB-O) provides a knowledge framework for FAIR model specification and automated code generation, while connecting model metadata to biomedical ontologies [@Martin2025].

TVB-Optim enables gradient-based fitting of large parameter spaces in whole-brain models and supports efficient scaling for computationally intensive optimization experiments [@Pille2025].

Relevant examples of mechanistic and translational whole-brain modeling include connectome-constrained decision dynamics [@Schirner2023], connectome-driven cortical wave phenomena [@Koller2024], and ODE-based separation of task and rest fMRI signals [@Kashyap2025].

Useful links:

- The Virtual Brain: https://thevirtualbrain.org
- TVB-O: https://virtual-twin.github.io/tvbo
- TVB-Optim: https://virtual-twin.github.io/tvboptim

## Format

Hands-on session introducing the theoretical and programmatic framework for specifying, running, analyzing, and optimizing dynamical brain network models.

## Agenda

### Day 1

1. Intro to The Virtual Brain and its applications

- Basic ideas and history
- Mathematical framework
- Applications in basic research
- Clinical applications

1. Defining a FAIR brain network model

- Mean field models (hands on)
- Networks of coupled dynamics (hands on)

1. Dynamical perspective: how to analyze a brain system

- How to perform and interpret bifurcation analyses

1. Parameter exploration and model optimization

- Brain network models as computational hypotheses of the brain
- Use-case A: mechanistic simulation of Alzheimer's disease and integration of biomedical data
- Use-case B: homogeneous vs. heterogeneous parameter fitting of frequency gradients (or another map from TVBase)

1. Stimulating the brain

- Visual and TMS-evoked potentials (JR)

### Day 2: Mini Hackathon

We help participants answer and gain insight into their own research questions. Bring your own data if possible.

## Target Audience

Researchers interested in computational neuroscience, including data scientists, clinicians, engineers, and generally curious participants. Prior TVB experience is helpful but not mandatory.

## Requirements

- Basic Python programming skills are recommended.
- Notebooks can be run online (EBRAINS account required).
- Limited support for local setups is also provided.

---

| ![](../img/Charite.png){fig-alt="Charite logo" width=30%} | ![](../img/BIH.png){fig-alt="BIH logo" width=30%} | ![](../img/BSS.png){fig-alt="BSS logo" width=30%} |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|

## References
